content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 2, 2016

Albert S. Yang
Senior Deputy City Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-172

Dear Mr. Yang:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Palo Alto Mayor Pat
Burt, City Councilmembers Marc Berman, Eric Filseth, and Karen Holman, and City Manager
James Keene (collectively, the "officials") regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and
not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or
Section 1090.

QUESTION

Assuming Section 87100 disqualifies the officials from taking part in the making of the
governmental decision on whether to amend the City of Palo Alto's Downtown Residential
Preferential Parking District Pilot Program Resolution, may the officials take part in that decision
pursuant to the "limited neighborhood effects" exception?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The "limited neighborhood effects" exception applies, and the officials may take part
in the decision, based on the facts presented.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-172
Page No. 2

FACTS

By way of background, in late 2014, the Palo Alto City Council adopted an ordinance
setting forth general criteria and procedures for the creation of residential preferential parking
districts ("RPP districts") within the City. (See PAMC Chapter 10.50.) Under the ordinance, the
City Council may designate an area within the City a RPP if: (1) non-resident vehicles substantially
interfere or may substantially interfere with the use of on-street or alley parking spaces by
neighborhood residents; (2) the interference by non-resident vehicles occurs at regular and frequent
intervals, either daily or weekly; (3) the non-resident vehicles parked in the area of the proposed
district create traffic congestion, noise, or otherwise disrupts neighborhood life; and (4) other
alternative parking strategies are not feasible or practical. (PAMC Chapter 10.50.030.)

The City then conducted parking surveys and obtained testimony indicating that the City's
downtown area had experienced sustained and substantial parking intrusion from non-resident
vehicles. On December 2, 2014, the City Council adopted the Downtown RPP District Pilot
Program Resolution based on this evidence, thereby establishing the Downtown RPP District and
regulating on-street parking within the District.

The City Council amended its RPP ordinance on February 1, 2016. The City Council also
deliberated over the performance of the Downtown RPP District Pilot Program to date, and
considered petitions from residents on nearby streets to annex those streets into the District. The
City Council then directed staff to prepare amendments to the Resolution. According to the City
Council Staff Report on the proposed amendments to the Resolution dated February 1, 2016, the
amendments would do all of the following:

• Update the Downtown RPP District boundaries to include newly annexed streets and approved
Eligibility Areas.

• Approve additional adjacent areas for future inclusion in the District by an administrative action
by the City's Planning and Environment Director upon an area's submission of a petition to be
annexed into the Downtown RPP District.

• Establish a maximum of 2,000 annual employee parking permits for the 2016 calendar year and
a method for allocating those permits throughout the different areas of the District.

You are a Senior Deputy City Attorney and the authorized representative of the officials.
Each official owns his or her primary residence within the existing Downtown RPP District.
According to the facts presented, the officials recused themselves from the prior discussions related
to RPP districts on December 2, 2014 and February 1, 2016. The City will soon consider whether to
amend the Resolution. You have requested advice regarding whether the "limited neighborhood
effects" exception applies, authorizing the officials to take part in the decision on whether to amend
the Resolution despite being disqualified under Section 87100.

File No. A-16-172
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 8100l(b).)

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's interests, and describes interests from which a conflict of
interest may arise under the Act. Thus, with respect to conflicts of interest under the Act, the
general rule is that a prohibited conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a
governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections
87100 and 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)

Regulation 18703(e)(3) sets forth the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, a specific
rule for special circumstances associated with the Act's "public generally" exception, and provides
as follows:

"(e) Specific Rules for Special Circumstances. The financial effect on a public
official's financial interest is deemed indistinguishable from that of the public
generally if the official establishes: …

"(3) Limited Neighborhood Effects. The decision affects residential real
property limited to a specific location, and the decision establishes, amends, or
eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic controls,
deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance or improve public safety, provided the body
making the decision gathers sufficient evidence to support the need for the
action at the specific location."

Thus, under the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, the "financial effect on a public official's
financial interest is deemed indistinguishable from that of the public generally" if the official
establishes: (1) the decision affects residential real property limited to a specific location; (2) the
decision establishes, amends, or eliminates ordinances that restrict on-street parking, impose traffic
controls, deter vagrancy, reduce nuisance, or improve public safety; and (3) the body making the
decision has gathered sufficient evidence to support the need for the action at the specific location.

The decision on whether to amend the City's Downtown RPP District Pilot Program
Resolution would affect residential real property limited to a specific location because the
Downtown RPP District includes residential neighborhoods in and around the City's downtown.
Therefore, the first element of the exception is met.

The Resolution established the Downtown RPP District and imposed on-street parking
restrictions within the District's boundaries, and the decision at issue will determine whether the
City amends the Resolution. Therefore, the second element of the exception is met.

File No. A-16-172
Page No. 4

Prior to adopting the Resolution on December 2, 2014, the City conducted parking surveys
and obtained testimony indicating that the City's downtown area had experienced sustained and
substantial parking intrusion from non-resident vehicles. In early 2016, the City Council considered
the performance of the Downtown RPP District Pilot Program to date, and staff was directed to
amend the Resolution in order to update the District's boundaries, revise elements of the Pilot
Program, and modify the methodology for distributing parking permits throughout the District.
Accordingly, the City Council has gathered sufficient evidence to support the need for amending
the Downtown RPP District Pilot Program Resolution, and the third and final element of the
exception is met.

In order for a public official to invoke the "limited neighborhood effects" exception, the
decision at issue must not have a unique financial effect on one or more of the official's financial
interests identified in Section 87103. (Beryl Advice Letter, No. I-15-196; Yang Advice Letter, No.
A-16-082.) In this case, the officials' interests are all primary residences within the Downtown RPP
District and there is no indication that the decision at issue will have a unique effect on these
interests as compared to other residences within the District.

Because all three elements of the "limited neighborhood effects" exception are met, and
because there is no apparent unique effect of the decision on any financial interest of any of the
officials, the exception applies based on the facts presented. Accordingly, the officials may take part
in the decision on whether to amend the Downtown RPP District Pilot Program Resolution.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

John W. Wallace
Assistant General [illegible]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl
